retrieved_docs.title: Forensic Odontology -- Clinical Significance -- Age Determination Based on Dental Data
retrieved_docs.content: Teeth are well known as the strongest structure of the entire human body and are thus extremely durable and identically resistant to external impacts like putrefaction, fire, explosions, and chemicals, etc., which brand them accessible for a broad post-mortem duration. Age can be assessed from the teeth by several techniques like the eruption of teeth, which is acknowledged as a good indicator of the age of the person. Other changes, which are noticeable with increasing age, are attrition, periodontal disease, secondary dentine formation, and root translucency, resorption of roots, root roughness, apposition of cementum, color change in the crown and the roots.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tooth Eruption -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The phenomenon of bone resorption offers a distinct pathway for the correct emergence and alignment of a developing tooth. The force propelling the tooth toward the occlusal plane along this path is believed to originate from bone apposition occurring apically to the developing tooth. [3]  The onset of eruptive movements typically coincides with the initiation of  root formation. [3] Root growth is not the primary cause of tooth eruption, although it may have a role in the process. This is evident as teeth can still erupt without fully formed roots. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Bone Age -- Introduction -- Techniques:
retrieved_docs.content: Atlas method: Based on an atlas containing standard age-matched orthopantomographic images, developed by Scour et al (1944); this was further modified by Moorrees et al and Anderson et al, where they defined dentition stages of all teeth. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Forensic Odontology -- Clinical Significance -- Age Determination Based on Dental Data
retrieved_docs.content: In the year, 1947 a technique was developed based on structural tooth changes by Gustafson. [8] According to his technique, there are six criteria. Those include attrition, amount of recession in gingiva, secondary dentin formation, apposition of cementum, resorption, and transparency of root. These alterations were itemized with a predetermined score ranging from zero (0) to three (3), and an equation put together for linear regression aimed at calculating the age of an individual. The relationship constant achieved was 0.98. This technique is considered as the standard for human age estimation and is being used ever since its development. The core shortcoming of Gustafson method was the setting of equality for all the standards for equivalency.

retrieved_docs.title: Bone Age -- Introduction -- Techniques:
retrieved_docs.content: Scoring method: Numerical method devised by Demirjian et al (1973), involving the maturity score of each tooth based on the level of dentition. A total maturity score is calculated by adding individual maturity scores. It has been demonstrated to show a good correlation with the GP method of bone age estimation. [27] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Forensic Odontology -- Clinical Significance -- Age Determination Based on Dental Data
retrieved_docs.content: Additionally, advanced methods were developed, which include the practice of using scanning electron microscopes and energy dispersive X-ray analysis ( SEM-EDXA) , an advanced technique to scan dentin for age estimation. Contemporary research from the United Kingdom observed the root length usage in estimating the age of a pediatric individual. Another method developed is known as the Average stage of attrition (ASA) method which is a clinical way of measuring the attrition of molar cusps; it measures the attrition of respective molar cusps and subsequently uses them in average calculations. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Forensic Odontology -- Clinical Significance -- Age Determination Based on Dental Data
retrieved_docs.content: Kashyap revised the Gustafson system using objective dimensions, removing the measures like the recession of gingiva and resorption of the root. He defined the age of a person by gauging the secondary dentin by its length which is the development of the pulp chamber and further accessing the root for the thickness of cementum formed. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Avulsed Tooth -- Treatment / Management -- Follow up Appointment
retrieved_docs.content: Severe damage to the neurovascular bundle and the periodontal ligament can lead to replacement root resorption or inflammatory resorption. These complications can be anticipated and possibly prevented with disinfectants at the time of reimplantation and systemic antibiotics. Resorption complications may need a root canal. [2] [17] [3] [18] If the extraoral dry time is over 90 minutes, soaking the tooth in agents such as fluoride may decrease resorption rates.

retrieved_docs.title: Avulsed Tooth -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Replantation is the treatment of choice, and time management and appropriate storage of the avulsed tooth are crucial factors that will guarantee a good prognosis. Anti-resorption therapy is a newer technique that prevents the common inflammation that avulsed teeth experience after replantation, using a combination of antibiotics and corticosteroids. Splinting and systemic antibiotics are also included in the treatment regime of a patient with an avulsed tooth. Finally, follow-up appointments are of utmost importance, where the tooth will be assessed clinically and radiographically to confirm tooth vitality and rule out root resorption or ankylosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Bone Age -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Calcaneal stages 0 to 2 corresponded to high chances of the contralateral slip in SCFE, and stage 3  corresponds closely to PHV. Visualization of cervical maturation: Schelgl et al (2017) described the role of EOS 2D/3D imaging in the assessment of bone age, based on cervical vertebral body morphologies (Hassel–Farman Bone Age Stages: Initiation, acceleration, transition, deceleration, maturation and completion stages). [26] Visualization of dental maturity: Predominantly studied for forensic purposes. Mineralization of teeth is much less affected by nutritional or endocrine disorders than the skeletal system.

retrieved_docs.title: Periodontal Disease -- Staging -- Staging
retrieved_docs.content: Staging reflects the severity of periodontitis and the complexity of its management. This is determined by factors such as CAL, radiographic bone loss, and tooth loss, with the classification based on the site with the most severe bone loss. The stages include: Stage I—Initial periodontitis: Bone loss is limited to the coronal third (≤15%). Stage II—Moderate periodontitis: Bone loss extends beyond 15% but remains less than 30%. Stage III—Severe periodontitis with potential for additional tooth loss: Bone loss reaches the mid-third of the root (30%–60%). Stage IV—Severe periodontitis with potential for loss of dentition: Bone loss exceeds 60% and extends to the apical third.

retrieved_docs.title: Avulsed Tooth -- Treatment / Management -- Follow up Appointment
retrieved_docs.content: If the tooth is non-vital, a root canal treatment is indicated since there is no probability of revascularisation. [9] If the pulp is vital, an x-ray should be taken using an individualized holder. During the consecutive follow-up appointments (after one, three, and six months), a new x-ray will be taken to check for resorption. After this point, if there is no visible resorption, the patient is recalled every year for follow-up. In case of resorption, a root canal treatment is indicated. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Forensic Odontology -- Clinical Significance -- Age Determination Based on Dental Data
retrieved_docs.content: Age determination is also formulated based on the degree of formation of crown and root structures, the stage of eruption, and the intermixture of primary and adult dentitions. It also helps in identification if an individual is having a comprehensive dental record and presence of any uniqueness like decay, malposition, overlapping, rotations, and restorations/fillings with different materials, diastema/gaps and dentures/Implants, etc.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Treatment / Management -- Root Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: If the coronal fragment has been repositioned and splinted, a clinical follow-up examination should be done after one week, four weeks (for splint removal), eight weeks, and one year. A clinical exam after one year is recommended if the coronal fragment has been extracted. Radiographic follow-up is only indicated where clinical findings are suggestive of pathosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Teeth -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Fusion may lead to crowding, occlusal disturbances, and esthetic problems. Since the fused teeth have a greater surface area and root mass, resorption may become further delayed, leading to delayed or ectopic eruption of its permanent successor.

retrieved_docs.title: Avulsed Tooth -- Treatment / Management -- Follow up Appointment
retrieved_docs.content: Immature teeth where the root has not yet wholly formed have a greater chance of revascularization after soaking in doxycycline. Primary teeth should not undergo replantation as this can damage the underlying permanent tooth germ.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 462
retrieved_docs.content: Other bone disease and musculoskeletal deformities are closely linked to inflammation (الالْتِهاب) and to bone resorption (resorpce kosti). These associations indicate that inflammatory processes and the loss of bone tissue are central mechanisms underlying a range of skeletal disorders and deformities. Recognizing the connection between inflammation and bone resorption can guide diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at preserving bone integrity and preventing further musculoskeletal complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2329
retrieved_docs.content: Infancy is identified as a specific developmental stage that belongs to the broader category of the period of life between birth and death, situating this early phase within the overall lifespan continuum.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 362
retrieved_docs.content: Other bone disease and musculoskeletal deformities are associated with bone resorption, indicating that these conditions often involve increased breakdown of bone tissue, which contributes to skeletal weakening and structural abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: diabetes mellitus, its specific complications (various forms of retinopathy and nephropathy), and the relationship between these complications and ocular destructive procedures is essential for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 256
retrieved_docs.content: trauma, surgery, age‑related degeneration, calcific deposition, congenital malformations, and metastatic cancer each generate distinct but often overlapping structural and functional disturbances in the posterior segment, necessitating comprehensive ocular assessment and targeted management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2387
retrieved_docs.content: The phrase “Due to” serves as a causal indicator and is associated with specific injury outcomes, including loss of teeth resulting from an accident (“verlies; tanden door ongeval”) as well as severe trauma such as crushing injuries or internal injuries. Recognizing this wording helps clinicians link the underlying cause to these particular clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 0 -- Community 19
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a dental procedure that directly targets teeth (zuby) and is recognized as part of the broader set of dental procedures. Dental procedures themselves are also performed directly on deciduous teeth (zuby mléčné). Disorders of teeth and jaw are categorized under the parent concept of teeth (zuby) and are specifically located in the deciduous dentition (zuby mléčné). Consequently, both the extraction of a single tooth and other dental procedures may be applied to permanent and baby teeth, while disorders of the teeth and jaw primarily affect the baby teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY is classified as a subcategory within the broader group of Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium, indicating that these health issues are primarily associated with the gestational period and its immediate aftermath.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 226
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are directly performed on zuby mléčné, indicating that primary (baby) teeth serve as the specific sites for these dental interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2431
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening in pregnancy is categorized as a component of other complications of birth and puerperium that influence the management of the mother, and it specifically encompasses the sub‑condition of cervical shortening occurring in the second trimester.
